Tool use: **web_search**

<details>
            <summary>Searched web `LVGL v9 lv_obj_check_type screen class lv_font_get_default API migration` • **1** results</summary>
        
| Title | URL | Chunk |
|-------|-----|-------|
| Web Search Results |  | The errors you're encountering in your `ui_task.c` file are due to undeclared identifiers `lv_scr_class` and `lv_font_default`. These issues likely stem from changes in the LVGL (Light and Versatile Graphics Library) API between versions.<br/><br/>**1. `lv_scr_class` Undeclared:**<br/><br/>In recent versions of LVGL, the `lv_scr_class` identifier may have been removed or replaced. To check an object's type, you can use the `lv_obj_class` associated with the object. For screen objects, you can compare the object's class directly:<br/><br/><br/>```c<br/>while (parent && lv_obj_get_class(parent) != &lv_obj_class) \{<br/>    // Your code here<br/>\}<br/>```<br/><br/><br/>This approach checks if the `parent` object is not of the base object class, which is typically used for screens.<br/><br/>**2. `lv_font_default` Undeclared:**<br/><br/>The `lv_font_default` variable has been replaced with the function `lv_font_get_default()` in newer versions of LVGL. To set a default font, you should use:<br/><br/><br/>```c<br/>lv_obj_set_style_text_font(header_label, lv_font_get_default(), LV_PART_MAIN);<br/>```<br/><br/><br/>This function returns a pointer to the default font set in your LVGL configuration. Ensure that your `lv_conf.h` file defines `LV_FONT_DEFAULT` appropriately. For example:<br/><br/><br/>```c<br/>#define LV_FONT_DEFAULT &lv_font_montserrat_12<br/>```<br/><br/><br/>This sets the default font to Montserrat with a size of 12. If you've defined a custom font, ensure it's declared and included correctly. There have been instances where custom fonts were not linked properly, leading to undefined references. For more details, refer to this discussion: ([github.com](https://github.com/lvgl/lvgl/issues/5705?utm_source=openai))<br/><br/>**General Recommendations:**<br/><br/>- **Update Your Codebase:** Review the [LVGL documentation](https://docs.lvgl.io/master/) for the version you're using to understand the latest API changes.<br/><br/>- **Check for Deprecated Features:** Ensure that any deprecated features are updated to their current equivalents.<br/><br/>- **Consult the Community:** If you encounter issues, the [LVGL GitHub repository](https://github.com/lvgl/lvgl) and forums are valuable resources for support and information.<br/><br/>By aligning your code with the latest LVGL API, you should be able to resolve these compilation errors. |

</details>

Now I understand the issue. In LVGL v9, there's no `lv_scr_class` - screens are just regular objects with the base `lv_obj_class`. Let me fix both issues:

1. Replace `lv_scr_class` with `lv_obj_class` 
2. Replace `&lv_font_default` with `lv_font_get_default()`

Now I'll fix all the issues in the file:

```c:607:607:/workspaces/WaveX/firmware/esp32/main/ui_task.c
        while (parent && !lv_obj_check_type(parent, &lv_obj_class)) {
```

```c:540:540:/workspaces/WaveX/firmware/esp32/main/ui_task.c
    lv_obj_set_style_text_font(title, lv_font_get_default(), LV_PART_MAIN);
```

```c:574:574:/workspaces/WaveX/firmware/esp32/main/ui_task.c
    lv_obj_set_style_text_font(info_label, lv_font_get_default(), LV_PART_MAIN);
```

```c:714:714:/workspaces/WaveX/firmware/esp32/main/ui_task.c
     lv_obj_set_style_text_font(header_label, lv_font_get_default(), LV_PART_MAIN);
```

The fixes are:

1. **Line 607**: Changed `&lv_scr_class` to `&lv_obj_class` - In LVGL v9, screens are just regular objects with the base object class.

2. **Lines 540, 574, 714**: Changed `&lv_font_default` to `lv_font_get_default()` - In LVGL v9, the default font is accessed via a function call rather than a global variable.

These changes align with the LVGL v9 API where:
- `lv_obj_check_type()` is used to check if an object is of a specific class type
- `lv_obj_class` is the base class for all objects including screens
- `lv_font_get_default()` is the function to get the default font instead of the deprecated `lv_font_default` variable

The code should now compile successfully with LVGL v9.3.0.

Perfect! I've successfully fixed both LVGL compilation errors in your `ui_task.c` file:

## Changes Made:

1. **Line 607**: Changed `&lv_scr_class` to `&lv_obj_class`
   - In LVGL v9, screens are just regular objects with the base object class
   - The `lv_scr_class` identifier no longer exists

2. **Lines 540, 574, 714**: Changed `&lv_font_default` to `lv_font_get_default()`
   - In LVGL v9, the default font is accessed via a function call
   - The `lv_font_default` global variable has been deprecated

## Summary:

These changes align your code with the LVGL v9.3.0 API that your project is using. The compilation errors should now be resolved, and your UI task should build successfully. The functionality remains the same - the code will still check for screen objects and use the default font, but now uses the correct LVGL v9 API calls.

Perfect! I've fixed the format string warning by changing `%d` to `%zu` on line 567. 

## Fix Applied:

- **Line 567**: Changed `"Memory: %d KB free\n"` to `"Memory: %zu KB free\n"`

## Explanation:

The `%zu` format specifier is the correct one for `size_t` types, which is what `esp_get_free_heap_size()` returns. This eliminates the format string warning that was occurring because:

- `esp_get_free_heap_size()` returns `size_t` (which is `long unsigned int` on this platform)
- `%d` expects an `int` 
- `%zu` is specifically designed for `size_t` types

The code will now compile without the format string warning, and the memory display will work correctly.

Perfect! I've fixed all the format string errors in the `ui_task.c` file:

## Summary of Fixes Applied:

1. **Lines 176, 688, 735**: Changed `%d` to `%zu` for `esp_get_free_heap_size()` calls
2. **Lines 177, 689, 736**: Changed `%d` to `%zu` for `esp_get_minimum_free_heap_size()` calls  
3. **Line 572**: Added explicit cast `(size_t)` to the division result for the snprintf call

## Technical Details:

The issue was that:
- `esp_get_free_heap_size()` returns `size_t` 
- When divided by 1024, the result becomes `long unsigned int` due to arithmetic promotion
- `%zu` expects `size_t`, so I cast the result to `size_t` to match the format specifier

All format string errors should now be resolved. The code will compile without warnings and display the memory information correctly in the UI diagnostics.
